title: Detecting Topological Sort in a Directed Acyclic Graph: Fix the Low Link Values

statement.markdown: Given a directed acyclic graph (DAG) represented as an adjacency list, write a function to perform a topological sort on the graph. If the graph has a cycle, the function should return False.
statement.inputFormat: An undirected or directed graph represented as a dictionary of adjacency lists.
statement.outputFormat: A list of nodes in topological order, or False if the graph has a cycle.
statement.notes: The graph may contain multiple nodes with the same name.

examples.explanation: Topological sort of the given graph.
Graph has a cycle, no topological sort possible.

hints.text: Use a recursive depth-first search (DFS) to traverse the graph.

editorial.title: Detecting Topological Sort in a Directed Acyclic Graph
editorial.intuition: A topological sort of a graph is an ordering of the vertices in the graph such that for every directed edge u -> v, vertex u comes before v in the ordering.
editorial.bruteForce: One approach is to use a recursive DFS to traverse the graph and keep track of the visited nodes.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use Kahn's algorithm to perform the topological sort.
editorial.optimalSolution: To solve this problem, we will use a recursive DFS to traverse the graph and keep track of the visited nodes.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proved by showing that the recursive DFS visits the nodes in the correct order.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run of the recursive DFS.
commonMistakes: Not initializing the visited array correctly.
Not handling the case when a node has no outgoing edges.
